back: Embeds AWS compute/storage at the edge of telecom providers' 5G networks, giving ultra-low-latency access for mobile/connected devices (e.g. AR/VR, real-time gaming, connected vehicles).

AWS's CDN — caches content at edge locations close to users to reduce latency and origin load, integrates with Shield/WAF for edge-level DDoS/application protection, and supports both static (S3) and dynamic (ALB/EC2/custom) origins, plus Lambda@Edge/CloudFront Functions for edge-side logic.

Frequently the front door in exam architectures needing global low-latency delivery plus security.
Dedicated, private physical network connection from on-premises to AWS, bypassing the public internet for consistent latency/bandwidth and reduced data transfer cost at scale.
AWS's managed load balancing service, automatically distributing incoming application traffic across multiple targets and scaling with demand.

Routes user traffic over AWS's private global backbone network to the optimal (closest/healthiest) regional endpoint using static anycast IPs, improving performance and providing fast failover between regions.

Exam distinction from CloudFront: Global Accelerator improves routing/failover for any TCP/UDP traffic (not just HTTP-cacheable content) and is often chosen for non-HTTP protocols or when static IPs are required, whereas CloudFront is specifically for caching HTTP(S) content at the edge.

Provides private connectivity from your VPC to supported AWS services, partner services, or your own services in another VPC, without traffic ever traversing the public internet or requiring VPC peering/route table changes.

Exam trigger: 'expose a service to another VPC/account without peering and without exposing it to the internet' — PrivateLink (via an interface endpoint) is the answer, distinct from VPC Peering, which fully connects two VPCs' networks.

Managed DNS service with built-in health checks and multiple routing policies for controlling how traffic is directed to your resources.

Choosing the correct routing policy for a given availability/performance/compliance requirement is a recurring exam pattern — see the individual routing policy cards for each option.

Routes traffic to a single resource, or returns multiple IP addresses in random order if several are specified (the client picks one) — no weighting, no health-based failover built into the policy itself.

The right choice when there's just one resource and no need for traffic control.

Splits traffic across multiple resources by an assigned relative weight (e.g. 70/30) — DNS responses are returned proportionally to those weights.

Common for A/B testing, canary/blue-green deployments, or gradually shifting traffic to a new environment.

Setting a record's weight to 0 stops traffic to it entirely without deleting the record.

Routes traffic to whichever Region gives a given user the lowest network latency, based on actual measured latency between users and AWS Regions — not physical distance.

Useful for global applications deployed across multiple Regions where performance is the priority; can be combined with health checks so an unhealthy Region is automatically excluded from answers.

Active-passive disaster recovery: designate a primary and a secondary record, each with an associated health check.

Traffic goes to the primary as long as its health check passes; if it fails, Route 53 automatically fails over to the secondary.

The go-to policy whenever a scenario says 'automatic failover to a DR site' without needing the sophistication of latency-based/multi-Region routing.

Routes traffic based on the geographic location of the user's DNS query (continent, country, or US state) — not network performance.

Used for content restriction/localization (serving region-specific content) or data-sovereignty compliance requirements that mandate users from a given country be served from resources located in that country.

Routes traffic based on the geographic location of your resources relative to users, with an optional 'bias' value that expands or shrinks the geographic region from which traffic is routed to a given resource, letting you shift more or less traffic toward a resource without physically moving it.

Configuring it requires Route 53 Traffic Flow, the visual policy editor.

Like Simple routing (returns multiple values, the client picks one), but with health checks built in — Route 53 only returns the IP addresses of currently healthy resources (up to 8 per response), giving lightweight, DNS-level load distribution plus failover.

Not a substitute for a real load balancer, but a common exam distinguisher from plain Simple routing, which has no health-check awareness at all.

Acts as a centralized network router: each VPC needs only one Transit Gateway attachment (regardless of how many other VPCs/networks it must reach), a Direct Connect Gateway attaches to the Transit Gateway to bring on-premises connectivity in through one centralized connection, and AWS RAM shares the Transit Gateway across accounts so new accounts/VPCs can be added without building a web of pairwise connections.

Your isolated virtual network — subnets (public/private, tied to an AZ), route tables, internet/NAT gateways, security groups (stateful, instance-level) and NACLs (stateless, subnet-level) are the core building blocks.

Exam-critical distinction: security groups only allow rules (implicit deny) and are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed); NACLs support explicit allow AND deny rules and are stateless (return traffic must be explicitly allowed too).

Encrypted IPsec tunnel(s) over the public internet connecting on-premises networks to a VPC (via a Virtual Private Gateway or Transit Gateway) — fast to provision (minutes) but subject to public internet variability, unlike Direct Connect's dedicated private line.

Commonly paired with Direct Connect as an encrypted backup path, or used alone for lower-cost/lower-urgency hybrid connectivity.
A Layer 2 device that forwards frames between devices on the same network segment based on MAC address, giving each connected device its own collision domain (unlike a hub).
A Layer 3 device that forwards packets between different networks/subnets based on destination IP address, using a routing table.

A Layer 1 device that simply repeats/broadcasts any incoming signal out to every other connected port, with no addressing intelligence — all connected devices share one collision domain, causing collisions and poor scalability.

Long obsolete, replaced by switches.

A Layer 2 device that connects two network segments and forwards frames between them selectively based on MAC address, reducing collision domains — historically the predecessor to modern multi-port switches.

A general term for any device or service that connects two different networks, often translating between protocols and potentially operating at any OSI layer up to Layer 7 — unlike a router (which just forwards within compatible network types), a gateway can bridge fundamentally different network models.

The set of rules (routes) that determines where network traffic from a subnet or gateway is directed, each route pairing a destination CIDR with a target (local, an Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPC Peering connection, Transit Gateway attachment, VPN, etc.).

Every route table always has an unremovable local route covering the VPC's full CIDR, enabling all subnets within the VPC to reach each other by default.

Notation for expressing an IP address range as a base address plus a prefix length (e.g. 10.0.0.0/16), where a smaller prefix number means a larger address range (/16 = 65,536 addresses, /24 = 256 addresses).

A VPC's CIDR block can range from /16 to /28, and every subnet's CIDR must fall entirely within its VPC's CIDR and must not overlap any sibling subnet's CIDR.
A segmented range of IP addresses within a VPC's CIDR block, and — unlike in traditional on-premises networking — every AWS subnet is tied to exactly one Availability Zone.

A horizontally scaled, redundant, highly available VPC component that enables bidirectional internet connectivity — one is attached per VPC (a 1:1 relationship), and it performs the 1:1 NAT translating each instance's private IP to its assigned public/Elastic IP for internet-bound traffic.

AWS manages it as fully redundant with no bandwidth to provision, so it's never the answer to a scaling or HA question.

front: Wavelength vs. Local Zones vs. Outposts — what's the difference?
back:
Wavelength specifically targets 5G carrier networks for latency-sensitive mobile/edge applications.

Local Zones are AWS-owned infrastructure placed closer to users generally.

Outposts extends AWS infrastructure into a customer's own on-premises data center.

front: Direct Connect vs. Site-to-Site VPN — what's the tradeoff?
back:
Direct Connect gives a dedicated private link — higher cost and lead time, but consistent performance.

Site-to-Site VPN is an encrypted tunnel over the public internet — fast to set up, variable performance.

A common pattern: use VPN as a fast-to-provision backup path while Direct Connect is being established.

front: ALB vs. NLB vs. GWLB — how do you choose?
back:
Application Load Balancer (Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, content-based routing, WebSocket support) — choose for path-based routing.

Network Load Balancer (Layer 4, ultra-high-performance, static IPs, preserves client IP) — choose when a static IP is needed.

Gateway Load Balancer — for inserting third-party virtual appliances (like firewalls) transparently inline.

front: Transit Gateway vs. VPC Peering — why does Transit Gateway scale better?
back:
VPC Peering is non-transitive and becomes unmanageable at scale — there's no routing through a peered VPC to reach a third one, and you need a new connection (and route table update) per pair.

Transit Gateway scales cleanly instead as a hub-and-spoke model.

front: Why can instances in the same VPC subnet always reach each other with no route table entry?
back:
Because a VPC subnet's underlying network fabric performs Layer 2 (switch-like) forwarding invisibly between ENIs in the same subnet — no routing needed.

Crossing subnets is what requires Layer 3 routing.

front: What is the VPC router, and where does it live?
back: An implicit router every AWS VPC has, sitting at the reserved first IP of every subnet's CIDR — it consults each subnet's route table to send traffic to another subnet, an Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPC Peering connection, VPN, or Transit Gateway attachment. 'Traffic isn't reaching X' troubleshooting starts here.

front: Does AWS have an equivalent to a network hub?
back:
No — AWS has no hub-like construct at all.

It's worth knowing purely as OSI-layer contrast to a switch (Layer 2, MAC-aware, separate collision domains per port), reinforcing why VPC subnet-level traffic isolation works the way it does.

front: What's the closest AWS analogy to a network bridge?
back: AWS has no literal 'bridge' service, but VPC Peering and Transit Gateway serve a conceptually similar role at Layer 3 — joining two otherwise-separate networks (VPCs) so traffic can pass between them, even though they operate one layer up from a classic Layer 2 bridge.

front: AWS overloads the term 'Gateway' — what does each one actually do?
back:
Internet Gateway (VPC-to-internet), NAT Gateway (outbound-only internet access for private subnets), Virtual Private Gateway (VPN endpoint on the AWS side), Transit Gateway (hub connecting many VPCs/on-premises networks), Direct Connect Gateway (attaches a DX connection to VPCs across regions/accounts), Storage Gateway (on-premises-to-S3/EBS bridge), and API Gateway (client-to-backend API front door).

Never assume 'Gateway' implies specific behavior — match the exact service name to the problem.

front: What makes a VPC subnet 'public' vs. 'private'?
back:
Purely which route table it's associated with (every subnet has exactly one at a time — the VPC's main route table by default).

Public: has a route to an Internet Gateway.

Private: doesn't (and typically routes outbound traffic through a NAT Gateway instead).

front: Why does an AWS /24 subnet only have 251 usable IP addresses, not 256?
back:
AWS reserves the first 4 and the last 1 IP address in every subnet (5 total) for internal use — network address, VPC router, DNS, future use, and the broadcast address (even though VPC doesn't support broadcast).

Separately: overlapping CIDRs between VPCs is the single most common blocker to VPC Peering or Transit Gateway connectivity, since overlapping ranges make routing ambiguous.

front: Why do highly available VPC architectures deploy subnets across multiple AZs?
back: Because every AWS subnet is tied to exactly one Availability Zone (unlike traditional on-premises networking) — so HA design requires at least one subnet per AZ, across multiple AZs, not just multiple subnets in one AZ.

front: Does attaching an Internet Gateway alone make a subnet public?
back:
No — the subnet's route table also needs a route sending 0.0.0.0/0 (or ::/0 for IPv6) traffic to the IGW, and the instance itself needs a public or Elastic IP assigned.

An IGW alone does nothing.

front: IGW vs. NAT Gateway — what's the traffic-direction difference?
back:
Internet Gateway allows direct inbound AND outbound traffic for resources with public IPs.

NAT Gateway only allows outbound-initiated traffic for private-IP resources — nothing can initiate a connection in.

A direct, 1:1 private network connection between exactly two VPCs, letting resources in either one talk to each other using private IP addresses — routed by adding an entry to each VPC's route table pointing at the peering connection, not through any gateway or single point of failure.

front: Why does VPC Peering become unmanageable at scale?
back:
It's non-transitive: if VPC A peers with VPC B, and B peers with C, A still cannot reach C through B — a direct A–C peering connection is required.

Connecting N VPCs so every pair can talk therefore needs N×(N-1)/2 separate peering connections, each with its own route table entries to maintain in every VPC involved — a full mesh that grows quadratically and becomes operationally unmanageable past a handful of VPCs.

Transit Gateway replaces this with a hub-and-spoke model: one attachment per VPC, no per-pair connections to create or route tables to update as VPCs are added or removed.

A subnet is 'public' purely because its associated route table has a route sending 0.0.0.0/0 (or ::/0) traffic to an Internet Gateway.

The resource itself also needs a public or Elastic IP for the internet to actually reach it — both conditions are required together.

Public subnets typically host internet-facing resources: ALBs, bastion hosts, and NAT Gateways.

A subnet whose route table has no route to an Internet Gateway, so resources in it have no direct inbound or outbound path to the internet.

Outbound-only internet access (e.g. software updates, calling external APIs) is typically provided via a NAT Gateway placed in a public subnet.

Private subnets host backend resources like databases and internal app servers that should never be directly internet-reachable.

The rules that determine where traffic from a subnet is directed, each pairing a destination CIDR with a target (local, Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, VPN, etc.).

Every subnet is associated with exactly one route table at a time, and that association — not anything inherent to the subnet — is what makes it public or private.

An Internet Gateway is a horizontally scaled, redundant, and highly available VPC component that provides internet connectivity.

Exactly one is attached to a VPC at a time, and it performs 1:1 NAT, translating an instance's private IP address to its assigned public or Elastic IP address for internet-bound traffic.

Attaching an Internet Gateway by itself does not make a subnet reachable from the internet: the subnet's route table also has to send traffic for 0.0.0.0/0 to the Internet Gateway, and the instance itself needs a public or Elastic IP address assigned to it.

A NAT Gateway is a managed service placed in a public subnet that lets resources in a private subnet initiate outbound internet connections without being directly reachable from the internet themselves — outbound-initiated traffic only, unlike an Internet Gateway, which also allows direct inbound.

It's highly available within a single AZ only, so for resilience, deploy one NAT Gateway per AZ rather than one for the whole VPC — routing every AZ through a single NAT Gateway creates a cross-AZ dependency, and cross-AZ data transfer cost, if that AZ fails.

A security group is a stateful, instance-level (ENI-level) virtual firewall that supports allow rules only, with an implicit deny for everything else, and automatically allows return traffic for any connection matching an allowed rule, with no separate rule needed for the response.

Multiple security groups can attach to one ENI at a time, in which case their rules are combined, and the most permissive rule wins.

A NACL is a stateless, subnet-level virtual firewall that supports both explicit allow and explicit deny rules, evaluated in numbered order, with the lowest number evaluated first and the first match winning.

Because it's stateless, return traffic must be explicitly allowed by a separate rule, unlike security groups, which auto-allow it.

Every subnet is associated with a NACL (the default NACL allows all traffic), and NACLs are useful for explicitly blocking a specific known-bad IP at the subnet level, something security groups can't do since they only support allow rules.

front: Why does a resource have both a private IP and a public IP?
back:
The private IP is always assigned and is what the instance's network interface actually has at the OS level — used for all internal traffic (within the VPC, over Direct Connect/VPN, via VPC Peering/Transit Gateway); it never changes for the life of the ENI.

The public IP is optional and only needed for direct internet reachability — the Internet Gateway performs 1:1 NAT between it and the instance's private IP, so the instance's OS never actually sees its own public IP.

Keeping them separate means internal traffic never consumes/exposes public IP space, and internet-reachability (a NAT/route-table concern) stays decoupled from the instance's stable internal network identity.

front: Why do we need a VPC?
back:
A VPC gives you a logically isolated section of AWS with full control over your own network — your own IP address range (CIDR), subnets, route tables, and gateways — plus layered security (security groups at the instance level, NACLs at the subnet level) and the ability to segment public-facing resources from private backend ones.

It's also the anchor everything hybrid attaches to: VPN, Direct Connect, Transit Gateway, and PrivateLink all connect into a VPC.

Historically AWS had 'EC2-Classic,' a flat, shared network with no isolation or custom IP control — VPC replaced it entirely, and today every EC2 instance launches into a VPC.

front: Does using the default VPC mean different AWS accounts can reach each other?
back:
No — the default VPC is created per AWS account, per Region (typically with CIDR 172.31.0.0/16), and every account's default VPC is completely isolated from every other account's, 'default' or not.

Since all accounts' default VPCs share the same default CIDR range, they'd actually have overlapping IPs if connected directly — which is exactly why any VPC-to-VPC connectivity (VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, PrivateLink, VPN) always requires an explicit setup step, regardless of whether the VPCs involved are default or custom. 'Default' only means AWS pre-configured it with a public subnet per AZ, an Internet Gateway, and a route to 0.0.0.0/0 — it carries no special interconnectivity privilege.

front: If two VPCs have overlapping CIDRs but are never connected, does that cause a problem?
back:
No — overlapping CIDRs are completely harmless as long as the VPCs stay unconnected, since each VPC's routing is self-contained and never has to consider the other's address space.

The collision only becomes a real problem the moment you try to connect them (VPC Peering, Transit Gateway, VPN): routing can no longer unambiguously decide where a packet for a shared address should go.

VPC Peering explicitly rejects connections between VPCs with overlapping CIDRs for exactly this reason — it's a 'sleeping' problem, invisible until someone tries to link the networks.

front: Does all traffic entering a VPC always pass through some kind of Gateway?
back:
No.

Traffic from the internet (Internet Gateway), on-premises VPN (Virtual Private Gateway), Direct Connect (Direct Connect Gateway), or many other VPCs (Transit Gateway) does pass through a gateway construct.

But VPC Peering is the notable exception: a peering connection is just a route table target (the peering connection ID), not a gateway object — traffic routes directly between the two VPCs' private IP spaces with no gateway in the path.

Interface VPC Endpoints (PrivateLink) are another exception — they appear as a plain ENI with a private IP inside your subnet, not a gateway.

And instance-to-instance traffic within the same subnet never leaves Layer 2, so it doesn't even reach the VPC router, let alone a gateway.

front: How do you plan for non-overlapping VPC CIDR ranges across an organization?
back:
Use Amazon VPC IP Address Manager (IPAM) — it centrally plans, tracks, and monitors IP address allocation across every account and VPC in an AWS Organization via CIDR pools (optionally nested by Region, business unit, or environment).

Teams request a CIDR from the pool rather than picking one arbitrarily, so IPAM automatically allocates a non-overlapping sub-range and gives org-wide visibility into utilization.

The underlying discipline that makes it work: pick a large top-level range up front (e.g. all of 10.0.0.0/8), partition it by a fixed scheme (Region/environment/account), reserve space for future growth, and never rely on the account default VPC's shared CIDR (172.31.0.0/16) for anything meant to interconnect.

front: VPC Peering is a direct link, not a routing mechanism
back:
A peering connection is a dedicated point-to-point tunnel between exactly two VPCs — not a device capable of relaying traffic onward to a third party.

A route table entry pointing at a peering connection only governs a single hop: 'traffic leaving me for this CIDR goes out this way.' What never happens is VPC B taking traffic that arrived via its AB peering connection and forwarding it back out via its separate BC peering connection — each peering connection only ever handles direct, single-hop delivery between its own two endpoints.

This is why peering is non-transitive, and exactly the gap Transit Gateway fills: it's actually built to relay/route between many attachments, which peering connections deliberately are not.

front: Does VPC Peering eliminate data transfer costs?
back:
No — it eliminates internet egress fees specifically, the priciest tier, since peered traffic never leaves AWS's network to hit the public internet.

But data transfer charges still apply on top: traffic within the same Availability Zone is free, traffic across AZs in the same Region is charged at standard inter-AZ rates, and traffic across Regions is charged at inter-Region rates — both cheaper than internet egress, but not free.

A common gotcha: people assume 'peering = free' and get surprised by the cross-AZ/cross-Region line item.
